summary: Win25H2Clean now has a strong current-build evidence chain for Win32PrioritySeparation as an observed registry surface: reversible 2 -> 38 -> 2 VM proof, live wmiprvse.exe Procmon reads for both states, and bounded rebooted benchmark runs. The raw 0x26 bitmask semantics remain repo interpretation rather than a modern Microsoft-published contract.

doc_source.primary_contract: Microsoft's Win32_OperatingSystem documentation proves the Win32PrioritySeparation registry path and the high-level WMI mapping for ForegroundApplicationBoost, QuantumLength, and QuantumType.
doc_source.repo_interpretation: The raw Win32PrioritySeparation bitmask interpretation for values such as 0x26 still comes from repo analysis and historical decompilation, not from a modern Microsoft page that publishes the exact bit semantics.
doc_source.notes: This re-audit keeps the Microsoft WMI contract separate from the repo-side raw scheduler interpretation.

ghidra.effect_summary: Legacy repo decomp exists for the read/write path, but no PDB-backed bounded v3.2 branch audit is attached to this record yet.

ida.effect_summary: IDA headless cross-verification is not provisioned for this record yet.

cross_verification.notes: Cross-verification is pending until a PDB-backed Ghidra branch audit and an IDA headless pass exist for this record.

setting.name: Foreground Scheduling Priority / Win32PrioritySeparation
setting.casual_explanation: This setting changes how Windows gives extra CPU time to the foreground application compared with background work. It is a low-level scheduler setting, not a normal everyday tweak.
setting.why_people_change_it: People change it when chasing old gaming or responsiveness advice, or when undoing manual scheduler tweaks from third-party guides.
setting.tradeoffs_overview: Changing the value can alter how much extra runtime the foreground app gets and how scheduler quantums behave. The feature area is real and the current build runtime evidence is strong, but the exact raw bitmask semantics for 0x26 still do not come from a modern Microsoft publication.
setting.professional_notes: Microsoft's Win32_OperatingSystem documentation maps Win32PrioritySeparation to HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\PriorityControl and exposes related high-level WMI properties such as ForegroundApplicationBoost, QuantumLength, and QuantumType. That page proves the path and the WMI-facing feature family, not the raw 0x26 bit semantics. Repo decompilation and historical notes still cover the raw bitmask interpretation, but this re-audit treats that interpretation as repo provenance. The Win25H2Clean VM then confirmed the current observed baseline value 2, the app profile 38 (0x26), and a clean restore back to 2 after real reboots. On 2026-03-26, a Procmon pass around `Get-CimInstance Win32_OperatingSystem` captured wmiprvse.exe reading Win32PrioritySeparation with Data: 2 and Data: 38 in separate reversible runs. That is strong enough to treat 2 and 38 as current observed states on this build, even though the exact raw 0x26 semantics remain repo interpretation rather than a Microsoft-published default.

allowed_values.label: Observed Win25H2Clean baseline
allowed_values.meaning: The current 25H2 VM baseline read back Win32PrioritySeparation = 2 before the app profile was applied. This is an observed runtime state, not a claim about the full raw bitmask semantics.

allowed_values.label: Observed app profile
allowed_values.meaning: The current app writes 38 (0x26), and the Win25H2Clean VM confirmed that observed state under real rebooted runs. This record does not claim that Microsoft publishes the full raw 0x26 bit semantics.

targets.notes: Tracked as a current observed scheduler state on this build, not as a Microsoft-published raw bitmask contract.

app_current_implementation.notes: Guest-side reversible probe and a later manual VM benchmark pass on Win25H2Clean confirmed the current app write and restore cycle for Win32PrioritySeparation.

windows_defaults.label: Observed Win25H2Clean baseline
windows_defaults.applies_to: Current 25H2 builds where the live baseline still reads back 2 before the app profile is applied

states.rationale: Use the current observed 25H2 VM baseline value.

windows_defaults.notes: This is the current observed VM baseline, not a claim about every Windows build.

recommended_profiles.label: Observed default

states.rationale: Use the current observed baseline value from the 25H2 VM.

recommended_profiles.good_when: You want the current observed 25H2 baseline for comparisons.
recommended_profiles.bad_when: You want the app's foreground-boost profile.
tradeoffs: Leaves the scheduler on the current observed baseline.
Still stays research-gated because the raw bitmask semantics are not fully published by Microsoft.

recommended_profiles.label: Current app profile

states.rationale: Use the app's observed 0x26 foreground scheduling profile.

recommended_profiles.good_when: You want to compare the app's current scheduler profile against the observed 25H2 baseline.
recommended_profiles.bad_when: You want only Microsoft-published raw bitmask values.
tradeoffs: The current app behavior is backed by Procmon, VM runtime, and bounded benchmark proof.
The exact raw scheduler semantics still are not published by Microsoft at the bit level.
recommended_profiles.notes: This is the current app-ready observed scheduler profile for the tested build, even though raw 0x26 still is not a Microsoft-published default.

evidence.title: Microsoft Learn: Win32_OperatingSystem class
evidence.summary: Microsoft maps ForegroundApplicationBoost, QuantumLength, and QuantumType to HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\PriorityControl\Win32PrioritySeparation. The page proves the path and high-level WMI contract, but it does not publish the exact raw 0x26 bitmask used by the app.

evidence.title: Repo system research notes for Win32PrioritySeparation
evidence.summary: The repo documents the raw bitmask interpretation for Win32PrioritySeparation and discusses values such as 0x18, 0x24, and 0x26, but this is repo provenance rather than a primary Microsoft publication.

evidence.title: Decompiled PriorityControl read/write path
evidence.summary: Repo decompilation shows a PriorityControl read/write path for Win32PrioritySeparation, but this is still repo static provenance rather than a PDB-backed v3.2 branch audit.

evidence.title: Procmon capture - wmiprvse.exe Win32PrioritySeparation reads
evidence.summary: Separate Win25H2Clean Procmon passes around `Get-CimInstance Win32_OperatingSystem` captured wmiprvse.exe reading HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\PriorityControl\Win32PrioritySeparation with Data: 2 and Data: 38 in reversible runs.

evidence.title: Current app implementation
evidence.summary: The provider writes Win32PrioritySeparation = 38 (0x26) for the current tweak.

evidence.title: Win25H2Clean reversible probe - Win32PrioritySeparation tuning
evidence.summary: Guest reversible probe changed Win32PrioritySeparation from 2 to 38 and restored 2, confirming the tuning value transitions on the PriorityControl path.

evidence.title: Win25H2Clean manual benchmark pass - Win32PrioritySeparation
evidence.summary: Manual VM pass confirmed baseline 2, candidate 38, and restored 2 after real reboots. The same guest then ran bounded WinSAT CPU + WPR and WinSAT mem + WPR passes for both states. The measured wall-clock durations were baseline CPU 35.28s vs candidate CPU 26.04s, and baseline mem 25.05s vs candidate mem 25.11s.

decision.why: Win25H2Clean reversible proof, the later rebooted VM benchmark pass, and the wmiprvse.exe Procmon reads for both states are enough to treat 2 and 38 as current-build observed runtime states. This verdict does not rely on Microsoft publishing the raw 0x26 bitmask semantics.

open_questions: Can a PDB-backed bounded branch audit replace the legacy repo decomp proof for Win32PrioritySeparation?
Can a modern Microsoft primary source for the exact raw Win32PrioritySeparation bitmask semantics be captured?

validation_proof.exact_quote_or_path: prioritycontrol-state-2.txt: wmiprvse.exe RegQueryValue ... Win32PrioritySeparation Data: 2. prioritycontrol-state-38.txt: wmiprvse.exe RegQueryValue ... Win32PrioritySeparation Data: 38. priority-control-20260324-201011/summary.json: baseline=2, candidate=38, restore=2 with bounded CPU and memory runs.
validation_proof.notes: The 2026-03-26 Procmon pass adds direct wmiprvse.exe reads for both observed states. The earlier rebooted VM benchmark pass still covers baseline 2, candidate 38, restore 2, and bounded CPU/memory measurements on the same build.
